items.question: I ___ my keys, so I can't open the door.
explanations: 'Lost' (past simple) is possible alone, but the result matters now ('I can't open the door'), which the present perfect shows best.
Correct! A recent action with a present result takes the present perfect: 'have lost'.
'Was losing' (past continuous) describes an action in progress, but losing keys is instant.
'Lose' is the base present form. The action is finished with a result now: 'have lost'.

items.question: We ___ to Paris last summer.
explanations: 'Last summer' is a finished time, so the present perfect is impossible. Use 'went'.
Correct! A finished-time expression like 'last summer' always takes the past simple: 'went'.
'Have been' is present perfect, which can't combine with 'last summer'.
'Go' is the base present form. A finished trip last summer takes 'went'.

items.question: ___ you ever ___ a famous person?
explanations: 'Ever' with a life experience (no specific time) calls for the present perfect, not the past simple.
Correct! Experience questions use 'Have + subject + ever + past participle': 'Have you ever met…?'.
'Have' is right, but the past participle of 'meet' is 'met', not 'meet'.
After 'did' we'd need the base form, and an unspecified experience takes the present perfect anyway.

items.question: She ___ at this school since 2018.
explanations: 'Worked' (past simple) suggests she doesn't work here now, but 'since 2018' says it continues.
Correct! A situation that started in the past and continues takes the present perfect with 'since': 'has worked'.
'Works' is a general present fact, but 'since 2018' links past to present, so use the present perfect.
'Was working' (past continuous) can't combine with 'since 2018' here. Use 'has worked'.

items.question: When ___ you ___ the news?
explanations: Correct! 'When' asks for a specific time, so it takes the past simple: 'When did you hear…?'.
'When' needs a specific moment, so the present perfect doesn't fit here.
After 'did' the verb is the base form: 'hear', not 'heard'.
'When have you hear' is not correct — 'when' requires the past simple: 'did you hear'.

items.question: He ___ his leg last week, so he can't play now.
explanations: Correct! Even though there's a result now, 'last week' is a finished time, which forces the past simple: 'broke'.
The result is present, but 'last week' is finished time — English uses the past simple: 'broke'.
'Breaks' is a present habit, which doesn't fit a single past event.
'Was breaking' describes an action in progress; breaking a leg is instant. Use 'broke'.

items.question: I ___ that film three times. It's my favourite.
explanations: 'Saw' needs a finished time. Here we count experiences up to now, so use the present perfect.
Correct! Experiences counted up to now ('three times') take the present perfect: 'have seen'.
'See' is the base present form and can't express a repeated past experience.
'Had seen' is past perfect, used for an action before another past moment — not the case here.

items.question: I've known her ___ ten years.
explanations: 'Since' needs a starting point (since 2015). 'Ten years' is a period, so use 'for'.
Correct! 'For' + a period of time (ten years). 'Since' would need a starting point.
'From' isn't used this way with the present perfect. The pattern is 'for' + period.
'During' says when something happened inside a period, not how long. Duration takes 'for'.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'This is my first visit to Rome.'
options: I went to Rome once before.
I have never been to Rome before.
I have been to Rome many times.
explanations: 'Went once before' contradicts 'first visit' — it would be your second time.
Correct! A first visit means no previous experience: 'I have never been to Rome before.'
'Many times' is the opposite of a first visit.
'Didn't go' says the visit never happened, but you are visiting now.

items.question: ___ you ___ your keys? We can't leave without them.
explanations: There's no finished time, and the result matters now, so the present perfect fits better than the past simple.
Correct! An action with a present result and no stated time takes the present perfect: 'Have you lost…?'.
'Have' is right, but you need the past participle 'lost', not 'lose'.
After 'did' we'd use the base form 'lose', but the present result calls for the present perfect here.
